
[Revised / Grounded & Paced]

Kel'Thuzad nodded once, formal enough to insult.

"That is the strongest version of the argument," he said. "Antonidas never put it so cleanly."

"Do not speak as though you knew him better than I do."

"I knew him before the Violet Citadel trusted you with a staff." Kel'Thuzad's pale eyes did not leave hers. "He taught you the same discipline he taught me. The difference is that I accepted where it led."

"No." Jaina's voice came quieter now, but harder. "The difference is that he understood the cost, and you chose not to."

Kel'Thuzad studied her. The green fungal light caught the severe, hollowed lines of his face. "Perhaps. And perhaps he understood the cost only because he lacked the will to bear it."

The argument had reached its floor. Standing across from him felt too much like taking instruction. Jaina turned and went back to the table. The heavy oak chair scraped harshly against the flagstone when she pulled it, but she sat, letting the lake-cold bleeding up through the soles of her boots steady her.

Kel'Thuzad let the silence stretch. He did not rush to follow her. When the quiet had held long enough to make the point, he took his own chair.

He folded one vellum page over another, exposing a new diagram. The geometry shifted. Wider structure. Multiple runic chains descending across the page, pulling inward from the margins to converge on a single, heavy coordinate. Jaina stared at the ink. The sheer density of the lattice made her stomach tighten. It wasn't a local binding. It hadn't been drawn for Andorhal, or the Eastweald, or even Lordaeron.

"You came for mechanism," he said. "You have it. You came for scale as well, though you did not know that when you broke the ward."
